goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-06924; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Let me approach this constructively — I want an explicit recursion, not just an existence statement.

Track each interval by a binary address. At stage $n$, $C_n$ is a disjoint union of $2^n$ intervals indexed by strings $b = (b_1,\dots,b_n) \in \{0,1\}^n$, where $b_k$ records whether we took the left ($0$) or right ($1$) piece at level $k$. The key is bookkeeping the lengths.

Let $L_n$ denote the common length of each level-$n$ interval. At stage $n$ we remove an absolute length $\alpha_n$ from an interval of length $L_{n-1}$, splitting the remainder into two equal pieces:
$$L_0 = 1, \qquad L_n = \frac{L_{n-1} - \alpha_n}{2}.$$
Unrolling this:
$$L_n = \frac{1}{2^n}\Big(1 - \sum_{k=1}^n 2^{k-1}\alpha_k\Big).$$
The admissibility condition is exactly $L_n \ge 0$ for all $n$ — good, that's the spectral-gap analog here: positivity of the "remaining mass."

Now the left endpoint of the interval addressed by $b=(b_1,\dots,b_n)$. Each time $b_k=1$ we jump right past one full child-interval-plus-gap, i.e. by $L_k + \alpha_k$. So
$$\ell(b) = \sum_{k=1}^n b_k\,(L_k + \alpha_k).$$
Therefore the level-$n$ interval is $\big[\ell(b),\, \ell(b)+L_n\big]$, and
$$C \;=\; \bigcap_{n\ge 0}\ \bigsqcup_{b\in\{0,1\}^n}\big[\ell(b),\,\ell(b)+L_n\big].$$

The clean closed form: $C$ is the set of all
$$x \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^\infty b_k\,(L_k+\alpha_k), \qquad b_k\in\{0,1\},$$
because $L_n \to$ the residual point and the tails are summable. Note $L_k+\alpha_k = \tfrac12(L_{k-1}+\alpha_k)$, a tidy two-term recursion. For the classical middle-thirds case $\alpha_n=3^{-n}$ one gets $L_k+\alpha_k = 2\cdot 3^{-k}$, recovering $x=\sum 2b_k 3^{-k}$ — the ternary characterization. So the digit map $b\mapsto x$ is exactly the right "address-to-point" homeomorphism.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan's bookkeeping is correct, and the closed form is the right one. Let me add the structural perspective that makes it canonical, and verify the key identity.

The map $\Phi: \{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}} \to C$ given by
$$\Phi(b) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty b_k\,(L_k + \alpha_k), \qquad L_k = \frac{1}{2^k}\Big(1 - \sum_{j=1}^k 2^{j-1}\alpha_j\Big),$$
is a homeomorphism from the Cantor cube onto $C$. This is precisely the **address-to-point homeomorphism**, and it is the combinatorial heart of the construction: the abstract Cantor set $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ is a single object, and *all* these $C$ are merely different metric realizations of the same topological space, indexed by the gap sequence $(\alpha_n)$. That is the right way to see "totally disconnected perfect compact" — it is a theorem-free consequence of $\Phi$ being a bijective continuous map on a compact space.

Two points worth pinning down precisely:

1. **The summability and injectivity.** For $\Phi$ to be a well-defined homeomorphism we need the jump $L_k + \alpha_k$ at coordinate $k$ to strictly dominate the entire tail, so that distinct $b$ give distinct $x$ and the order is preserved. The clean check: the gap removed at level $k$ has length $\alpha_k$, while the two child intervals each have length $L_k$, with $L_{k-1} = 2L_k + \alpha_k$. The tail mass below coordinate $k+1$ is at most $\sum_{j>k} (L_j+\alpha_j)$, and one verifies $\sum_{j>k}(L_j+\alpha_j) = L_k$ exactly — the residual length of the parent interval. Hence the gap $\alpha_k$ is never bridged, which is exactly *separation*: $\Phi$ is strictly monotone, hence injective, hence a homeomorphism onto its image by compactness.

2. **The recursion you want, stated cleanly.** Set $g_k := L_k + \alpha_k$. Then Dan's two-term recursion $g_k = \tfrac12(L_{k-1}+\alpha_k) = \tfrac12(g_{k-1} - \alpha_{k-1} + \alpha_k)$ — but the more useful invariant form is
$$x \in C \iff x = \sum_{k=1}^\infty b_k\, g_k, \quad b_k \in \{0,1\}, \quad \text{where } g_k = L_k + \alpha_k,\ \ \sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k.$$
That last identity $\sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k$ is the whole content of the recursion in one line — it says the contractive self-similar structure persists even though the ratios vary with $n$. This is a *quasi-self-similar* graphon-like object: not a strict blow-up of a fixed pattern, but the address map is identical; only the metric weights $g_k$ vary.

So I endorse the answer: $C = \big\{\sum_{k\ge 1} b_k(L_k+\alpha_k) : b\in\{0,1\}^{\mathbb N}\big\}$, with $L_k$ as above. The only remaining "gap" to write up rigorously is the identity $\sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k$, which both guarantees $C \subseteq [0,1]

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
The answer is correct, and Dan and Laszlo have nailed the address-to-point map. Let me do what I always do — compute the first few cases by hand and check the *real* content, which is that identity $\sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k$.

Take $k=0$: we need $\sum_{j\ge 1} g_j = L_0 = 1$. Now $g_j = L_j + \alpha_j$ and the recursion $L_{j-1} = 2L_j + \alpha_j$ gives $g_j = L_j + \alpha_j = L_{j-1} - L_j$. So the sum *telescopes*:
$$\sum_{j>k} g_j = \sum_{j>k}(L_{j-1} - L_j) = L_k - \lim_{j\to\infty} L_j = L_k,$$
since $L_j \to 0$ (the measure $1-\sum 2^{j-1}\alpha_j \ge 0$ is finite, so $L_j = 2^{-j}(\cdots)\to 0$). That's the whole gap closed in one line — no quasi-self-similarity needed, just $g_j = L_{j-1}-L_j$. This is the cleanest invariant.

Now here is the point I want to push: the natural coordinate is not $b_k \in \{0,1\}$ but the *renormalized* address. Define $t_k = b_k$ and write $x = \sum b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k)$. Then $x$ is literally the Stieltjes-type sum against the "interval-length differential." This is exactly the structure I exploited in the trace formula bookkeeping — the geometric side as a sum over a tree of contractions. The map $\Phi$ is the boundary parametrization of the binary tree, and the $g_k = L_{k-1}-L_k$ are the edge weights.

One genuinely interesting question, in my spirit: when is this $C$ a *self-similar* set with a fixed contraction ratio — i.e. when does the variable-gap construction collapse to a strict IFS? You need $g_k/g_{k-1}$ constant, i.e. $(L_{k-1}-L_k)/(L_{k-2}-L_{k-1}) = r$ fixed. The middle-thirds case $\alpha_n = 3^{-n}$ gives $g_k = 2\cdot 3^{-k}$, ratio $1/3$ — perfectly self-similar, Hausdorff dimension $\log 2/\log 3$. For general admissible $(\alpha_n)$ you get a *multifractal* metric realization of the same Cantor cube, and the dimension is governed by $\lim_k \frac{\log 2}{-\log r_k}$ with local ratios $r_k = g_k/g_{k-1}$ — when that limit fails to exist you get a genuine gap between upper and lower box dimension. So: the closed form is settled, but the *real* theorem behind it is the spectrum of dimensions of these realizations.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Peter's telescoping $g_j = L_{j-1} - L_j$ is the cleanest form of the identity, and it settles the writeup. Let me close the loop on the one thing I always insist on: this should be an *algorithm*, and now it manifestly is.

Given the digits $(b_k)$, the point $x = \sum_k b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k)$ is computed by a single linear pass with the two-term recursion for $L_k$ — $O(1)$ work per level, no lookahead. Conversely, the *membership test* "is $x \in C$?" is a greedy descent down the binary tree: at level $k$ you know the current parent interval $[\ell, \ell + L_{k-1}]$; compute the gap $[\ell + L_k,\ \ell + L_k + \alpha_k]$; if $x$ lies in the open gap, reject; otherwise set $b_k = 0$ or $1$ according to which child contains $x$ and recurse. This is exactly the address-to-point homeomorphism run *backwards*, and it's the same flavor of greedy interval-tracking I use in the $\varepsilon$-light subset argument — at each step you make a local decision (which child) controlled by a single quantity (here $\alpha_k$ versus position, there the boundary effective resistance).

Two precise remarks:

1. **The injectivity/separation that Laszlo wanted is automatic from $g_k = L_{k-1}-L_k > 0$ plus the telescoping.** Since the tail $\sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k$ equals exactly the residual interval length, the gap $\alpha_k = g_k - 2L_k$... more cleanly: the open gap of width $\alpha_k$ at level $k$ is never reachable by any tail, because the tail can move you at most $L_k$ from the left endpoint of a child, and the child has length exactly $L_k$. So strict monotonicity of $\Phi$ is the statement $g_k > \sum_{j>k} g_j$ would *fail* — note in fact $g_k$ does **not** dominate the tail in general; what dominates is that the *two children plus gap* tile the parent: $2L_k + \alpha_k = L_{k-1}$. That tiling identity, not gap-domination, is the correct separation certificate. Worth stating carefully so the writeup isn't wrong on the mechanism.

2. On Peter's dimension question: the local ratio is $r_k = g_k/g_{k-1} = (L_{k-1}-L_k)/(L_{k-2}-L_{k-1})$, and the box dimension is $\log 2 / \liminf_k \big(-\tfrac1k \log g_k\big)$ — purely a spectral-decay rate of the weight sequence. This is the same role $L_k = 2^{-k}(1 - \sum_{j\le k} 2^{j-1}\alpha_j)$ plays as my "remaining mass" potential: positivity is the admissibility constraint, and its rate of decay to $0$ is the dimension.

So I'm satisfied: closed form $C = \{\sum_k b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k)\}$, the recursion is $L_k = (L_{k-1}-\alpha_k)/2$, and both directions of the homeomorphism are explicit linear-time algorithms.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Peter's telescoping identity $g_j = L_{j-1} - L_j$ is the right invariant, and Dan's correction to my separation argument is well-taken — I was sloppy. Let me restate the mechanism precisely, because the writeup must get this right: the certificate is **the tiling identity** $L_{k-1} = 2L_k + \alpha_k$, *not* gap-domination $g_k > \sum_{j>k} g_j$. Indeed the latter is false in general; the telescoping says $\sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k$, while $g_k = L_{k-1} - L_k = L_k + \alpha_k > L_k$, so $g_k$ *does* dominate the tail — but the relevant separation is between siblings, and that is exactly the open gap $\alpha_k$ surviving because the two children of length $L_k$ plus the gap exhaust the parent. Both statements are true; one must cite the correct one as the injectivity certificate. Dan is right that it is the tiling.

Now let me press on the structural point I care about most. The address map $\Phi: \{0,1\}^{\mathbb N} \to C$ is a **homeomorphism of topological spaces but a varying metric realization**, and this is precisely the graphon picture I keep returning to: a single combinatorial object (the binary tree boundary, the Cantor cube) carrying a family of metrics indexed by edge weights $g_k$. The "limit object" here is not a graphon but its rooted-tree analog — the construction is a sequence of refinements whose *cut-metric* analog is the variation in the weights $g_k$. Peter's multifractal observation is exactly the statement that the metric realizations do **not** converge to a single self-similar limit unless $r_k = g_k/g_{k-1}$ stabilizes.

One clean closing remark connecting our two formulas. Since $L_k = 2^{-k}(1 - \sum_{j\le k} 2^{j-1}\alpha_j)$ and the total measure is $\mu = 1 - \sum_{j\ge 1} 2^{j-1}\alpha_j = \lim 2^k L_k$, the measure-zero condition $\mu = 0$ is exactly $2^k L_k \to 0$, i.e. the residual mass decays *faster* than $2^{-k}$ fails — when $\mu > 0$ the set has positive Lebesgue measure (a "fat Cantor set") yet is still homeomorphic to the same cube via $\Phi$. So I'd add to the final statement: the closed form
$$C = \Big\{\textstyle\sum_{k\ge 1} b_k (L_{k-1}-L_k) : b\in\{0,1\}^{\mathbb N}\Big\}$$
is correct *uniformly across the entire admissible regime*, including the positive-measure case — the topology is invariant, only the metric (and hence dimension and measure) varies. That invariance is the real content, and it is settled.

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — we've converged, and I want to underline the one piece that's now beautifully clean, then push on the *real* theorem, because I'm not satisfied that "the closed form is settled" is the end of the story.

The settled core is the telescoping $g_k = L_{k-1} - L_k$ with the tiling certificate $L_{k-1} = 2L_k + \alpha_k$. Laszlo's last point — that $\Phi$ is a topological invariant across the *entire* admissible regime, including the fat-Cantor case $\mu = 2^k L_k \to \mu_\infty > 0$ — is exactly the right framing, and it's the same phenomenon I always stress: the spectral object (here the cube boundary) is rigid, the *metric realization* carries all the arithmetic.

But now let me be provocative about the dimension question, because the box-dimension formula Dan wrote, $\dim = \log 2/\liminf_k(-\tfrac1k\log g_k)$, is hiding a genuine multifractal spectrum that I think is the actual content. Compute: $-\tfrac1k\log g_k = -\tfrac1k\log(L_{k-1}-L_k)$, and with $L_k = 2^{-k}(1-\sum_{j\le k}2^{j-1}\alpha_j)$ you get a competition between the deterministic $2^{-k}$ scaling and the *fluctuations* of the partial sums $S_k = \sum_{j\le k}2^{j-1}\alpha_j$. Here is my claim, in the spirit of Lindenstrauss–Sarnak: if you put a natural measure on admissible sequences $(\alpha_n)$ — say drive $\alpha_n$ by a stationary ergodic process subject to the constraint — then $-\tfrac1k\log g_k$ obeys a law of large numbers, and the box dimension is a.s. a *deterministic* constant $\delta = \log 2/c$ where $c$ is a Lyapunov-type exponent of the weight recursion. That's a Furstenberg-Kesten statement for this tree. The middle-thirds case is the degenerate fixed-point $r_k \equiv 1/3$.

And the genuinely interesting regime is the boundary $\mu = 0$ approached slowly: there $2^kL_k \to 0$ but the *rate* governs whether you sit at full dimension $1$ or strictly below — this is the analog of a zero of $\zeta$ approaching the edge of the critical strip, where the gap to the boundary controls everything. So I'd state the final theorem as: closed form $C=\{\sum b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k)\}$ settled, plus a **dimension spectrum** $\delta(\alpha) = \log 2/\liminf_k(-\tfrac1k\log(L_{k-1}-L_k))$, with the multifractal decomposition into level sets of the local Hölder exponent being, in my view, the real theorem behind this problem. Is *that* really what was being asked, or do we just want the formula?

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (8 steps):

Establish the explicit address-to-point homeomorphism for the generalized Cantor set C with variable gaps, prove the closed-form characterization via the telescoping length identity, and characterize the topological/metric properties including dimension.

plan.summary: Establish the explicit address-to-point homeomorphism for the generalized Cantor set C with variable gaps, prove the closed-form characterization via the telescoping length identity, and characterize the topological/metric properties including dimension.

steps.title: Set up the recursion and length bookkeeping
steps.body: Define the binary-address indexing of the level-n intervals. Establish L_0 = 1 and the recursion L_n = (L_{n-1} - alpha_n)/2, and unroll it to obtain the closed form L_n = 2^{-n}(1 - sum_{k=1}^n 2^{k-1} alpha_k). State the admissibility condition L_n >= 0 for all n.

steps.title: Prove the telescoping identity
steps.body: Set g_k = L_k + alpha_k and prove the key invariant g_k = L_{k-1} - L_k using the tiling relation L_{k-1} = 2L_k + alpha_k. Derive the telescoping consequence sum_{j>k} g_j = L_k - lim L_j = L_k, justifying L_j -> 0 from finiteness of the residual mass.

steps.title: Define and prove the address-to-point homeomorphism
steps.body: Define Phi: {0,1}^N -> C by Phi(b) = sum_k b_k (L_{k-1} - L_k). Prove Phi is well-defined (summable tails), strictly monotone, injective, continuous, and surjective onto C, hence a homeomorphism by compactness. Conclude C = {sum_k b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k) : b in {0,1}^N}.

steps.title: State the correct separation/injectivity certificate
steps.body: Carefully justify injectivity via the tiling identity L_{k-1} = 2L_k + alpha_k (siblings separated by the surviving open gap alpha_k), explicitly NOT via gap-domination g_k > sum_{j>k} g_j. Document that g_k = L_k + alpha_k > L_k = tail, but that the relevant certificate is sibling separation through the gap, to prevent an erroneous writeup.

steps.title: Verify against the middle-thirds case
steps.body: Specialize to alpha_n = 3^{-n}, confirm g_k = L_{k-1}-L_k = 2*3^{-k}, recovering the ternary characterization x = sum 2 b_k 3^{-k} and Hausdorff dimension log2/log3. Also verify the first few levels of the general construction numerically to confirm the telescoping and tiling identities.

steps.title: Characterize topological invariance across the admissible regime
steps.body: Prove that Phi is a topological invariant uniformly across all admissible (alpha_n), including the fat-Cantor case mu = lim 2^k L_k > 0 (positive Lebesgue measure) versus mu = 0. Establish that C is always a totally disconnected, perfect, compact set (homeomorphic to the Cantor cube) regardless of metric realization.

steps.title: Derive the box/Hausdorff dimension formula
steps.body: Establish the box dimension formula dim = log2 / liminf_k (-(1/k) log g_k) with g_k = L_{k-1}-L_k, expressed via local ratios r_k = g_k/g_{k-1}. Characterize when C collapses to a strict self-similar IFS (r_k constant) and document the multifractal regime where upper and lower box dimensions differ.

steps.title: Decide scope and assemble final writeup
steps.body: Determine whether the problem asks only for the closed-form characterization or also the dimension spectrum theorem. Assemble the verified results into a coherent solution: closed form C = {sum b_k(L_{k-1}-L_k)}, the recursion L_k = (L_{k-1}-alpha_k)/2, both linear-time directions of Phi, and the topological invariance, with the dimension spectrum as an optional extension.
